content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441              322-6444

January 17, 1983


Steven A. Amerikaner
City Attorney
City of Santa Barbara
P.O. Drawer P-P
Santa Barbara, CA 93102

Re: Our Advice File No. A-83-003

Dear Mr. Amerikaner:

     Thank you for your letter of December 29, 1982, requesting
advice on the interpretation of Government Code Section 84308.
You asked whether a member of a quasi-judicial board or commission who is not also a public officer in some other capacity is
covered by subsection (a) of Section 84308.  It is our advice
that such a member (or alternate) is covered by subsection (a)
as well as by subsection (b) of Section 84308.

     You noted in your letter that subsection (a) uses the phrase
"elected or appointed officer, or alternate, or candidate for
office", and the first sentence of subsection (b) uses the
phrase "member and alternate".  We do not believe that the
difference in language in these two subsections was intended to
have any significance.  On the contrary, it is our view of the
legislative intent that all members of quasi-judicial boards or
commissions were intended to be covered by both subsection (a)
and subsection (b).

     In interpreting this section, it should be noted that
although the first sentence of subsection (b) refers only to
members and alternates, which requires
disqualification, refers to "elected or appointed officer or
alternate or candidate for office", as does the second paragraph
of subsection (b).  Subsections (c) and (d) also appear to use
the two terms interchangeably.  Finally, since Section 84308 is
part of the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections
81000, et seq.), the definitions of the Act apply to its
provisions.  Government Code Section 82048 defines the term

Steven A. Amerikaner
January 17, 1983
Page Two


"public official" as including "members" of state or local
government agencies.  Thus, a "member" of a quasi-judicial board
or commission is an "official" within the meaning of the Act.
See also Government Code Sections 82041 and 82049.

I hope this has answered your questions.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:km

CITY OF SANTA BARBARA

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY                                    CITY HALL
                                                               DE LA GUERRA PLAZA
                                                               P.O. DRAWER 9-P
                                                               SANTA BARBARA, CA 93102
                                                               (805) 963-0611

December 29, 1982


Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq.
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

     Re:  Government Code §84308 (Chap. 1049, Stats 1982)

Dear Ms. Milman:

     We are writing to solicit your views on the proper inter-
pretation of certain language found in new Government Code
§84308.

     Specifically, in subdivisions (a) and (b), the phrase "elected
or appointed officer, or alternate, or candidate for office"
appears.  Persons who fall within the scope of this language
are subject to fairly stringent rules prohibiting accepting
campaign contributions under certain circumstances and,
where contributions have been accepted, requiring disquali-
fication from voting.

     By contrast, the first sentence of subdivision (b) applies a
somewhat less stringent rule to a person who is a "member
and alternate acting in the capacity of a member" sitting on
a quasi-judicial board or commission.  It appears to us that
the drafters of this statute intended there to be a distinc-
tion drawn between "an officer" and a "member" on a commission.

     The resolution of this issue is of great concern to the City
of Santa Barbara, as well as many other local governments,
inasmuch as our community has numerous "quasi-judicial
boards and commissions" on which citizens volunteer to serve
without pay.  Frankly, it is difficult to see how a volunteer
citizen commissioner can be said to be an "officer" of the
City, rather than simply a "member" of the board or commission.
Rather, it would appear that the word "officer" was intended
to apply to a person occupying an "office" in a community
(e.g. City Councilmember) who is also a member of a quasi-
judicial body (e.g. the Coastal Commission).

Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq.                -2-                December 29, 1982




     As you can imagine, the meaning ascribed to the words
"officer" and "member" will determine the extent to which
various citizen commissioners will be obliged to comply with
the complex rules set forth in this new statute.  Therefore,
expeditious resolution of this apparent ambiguity is very
desirable.

     Thank you for your prompt consideration.  I would be pleased
to discuss this matter with you at any time period.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        STEVEN A. AMERIKANER
                                        City Attorney

SAA:cr

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance      Administration        Executive/Legal        Enforcement        Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662           322-5660             322-5901               322-6441            322-6444


January 13, 1983


Steven A. Amerikaner
City Attorney
City of Santa Barbara
P.O. Drawer P-P
Santa Barbara, CA 93102

Re: A-83-003

Dear Mr. Amerikaner:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice under the
Political Reform Act. If you have any questions about your
advice request, please contact me directly at (916)
322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

sections.question: You asked whether a member of a quasi-judicial board or commission who is not also a public officer in some other capacity is covered by subsection (a) of Section 84308.
sections.conclusion: It is our advice that such a member (or alternate) is covered by subsection (a) as well as by subsection (b) of Section 84308.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of quasi-judicial boards or commissions who are not also public officers in another capacity are covered by Government Code Section 84308(a).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Members of quasi-judicial boards and commissions are covered by both subsections (a) and (b) of Section 84308, and the difference in language between the subsections was not intended to create any distinction in coverage.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document addresses statutory interpretation of Government Code Section 84308 regarding coverage of quasi-judicial board members. Letter dated January 17, 1983 in response to December 29, 1982 inquiry.

QUESTION: You asked whether a member of a quasi-judicial board or commission who is not also a public officer in some other capacity is covered by subsection (a) of Section 84308.

CONCLUSION: It is our advice that such a member (or alternate) is covered by subsection (a) as well as by subsection (b) of Section 84308.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that members of quasi-judicial boards or commissions are covered by both subsections (a) and (b) of Government Code Section 84308, notwithstanding the different terminology used in those subsections. This interpretation is supported by the legislative intent and the definitions in the Political Reform Act.
